question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Medium: Impacts caused by griefing with no economic damage to any user on the network'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the Allow.Errors field in NetworkOptions() response directly references the errors.Errors global slice pointer, and by calling /network/options at high frequency, cause the response serializer to repeatedly serialize the same large error list, consuming CPU and bandwidth?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Medium: Impacts caused by griefing with no economic damage to any user on the network'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of response caching on /network/status to force the server to re-query the DB for genesis and latest block on every request, even when the blockchain state has not changed, causing unnecessary DB read amplification?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Medium: Impacts caused by griefing with no economic damage to any user on the network'] [Function: NetworkList()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests to /network/list with HTTP keep-alive connections, holding open many persistent connections simultaneously to exhaust the server's file descriptor limit, preventing new connections from legitimate users?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Medium: Impacts caused by griefing with no economic damage to any user on the network'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that addressBookEntryRepo.Entries(ctx) is called on every /network/status request with no TTL-based caching at the networkAPIService layer, causing the address book to be re-fetched from DB on every call even though it changes infrequently, amplifying DB load?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Medium: Impacts caused by griefing with no economic damage to any user on the network'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that n.operationTypes slice is shared across all concurrent requests in NetworkOptions() with no copy-on-read protection, and if any code path appends to it concurrently, cause a data race that corrupts the slice for other concurrent callers?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Medium: Impacts caused by griefing with no economic damage to any user on the network'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user send /network/status requests with HTTP/1.1 pipelining to queue multiple requests on a single connection, bypassing per-connection concurrency limits and amplifying the number of concurrent DB operations triggered?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Medium: Impacts caused by griefing with no economic damage to any user on the network'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the context passed to RetrieveGenesis(ctx), RetrieveLatest(ctx), and addressBookEntryRepo.Entries(ctx) is the original request context with no added timeout, meaning a slow DB will hold the request goroutine open for as long as the DB takes, enabling goroutine leak under sustained load?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Medium: Impacts caused by griefing with no economic damage to any user on the network'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NetworkOptions() returns the full list of all supported operation types (n.operationTypes including types.OperationTypeFee) with no filtering or access control, enabling an attacker to enumerate all valid operation types and craft maximally disruptive requests to other endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Medium: Impacts caused by griefing with no economic damage to any user on the network'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user send a flood of /network/status requests that each trigger a full address book scan via addressBookEntryRepo.Entries(ctx), and by doing so cause DB I/O saturation that slows down the importer service writing new blocks, causing the reported CurrentBlockIdentifier to fall behind the actual chain tip?
